content.problemStatement:
There is a 10×1010 \times 1010×10 grid with rows numbered 111 to 101010 from top to bottom, and columns 111 to 101010 from left to right. Each cell is identified by a pair (r, c)(r, c)(r, c) which means that the cell is located at row rrr and column ccc.

If Chef's current location is (a,b)(a,b)(a,b), then in one move Chef can go to (c,d)(c,d)(c,d) if **both** of the following are satisfied:


a≠ca \neq ca=c
b≠db \neq db=d
If the starting cell and ending cell are the same, Chef is already at the destination and the answer is 0 moves.

Determine the minimum number of moves required to go from (sx,sy)(s_x, s_y)(sx​,sy​) to (ex,ey)(e_x, e_y)(ex​,ey​).


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first and only line of each test case contains four integer sxs_xsx​, sys_ysy​, exe_xex​, eye_yey​ — the coordinates of the starting and ending cells.


Output Format
For each testcase, output the minimum number of moves required to go from (sx,sy)(s_x, s_y)(sx​,sy​) to (ex,ey)(e_x, e_y)(ex​,ey​).
constraints: (sx, sy) may be equal to (ex, ey); in that case the answer is 0.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four integers sx, sy, ex, ey — the starting row, starting column, ending row and ending column respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line — the minimum number of moves required to reach (ex, ey) from (sx, sy).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: rows 1→9 and columns 2→8 both differ → 1 move.
Test case 2: same row (5) → need 2 moves.
Test case 3: rows 8→6 and columns 6→8 both differ → 1 move.
Test case 4: same column (10) → need 2 moves.

First case start equals end → 0 moves.
Second case both coordinates differ → 1 move.
Third case same row (6) → 2 moves.

solution.approach:
From any cell you can move to any other cell that does **not** share its row nor its column. Therefore:
- If the start and end cells are identical, 0 moves are needed.
- If both the row and the column differ (sx != ex && sy != ey), you can jump directly in one move.
- Otherwise (sx == ex or sy == ey) a direct move is impossible, but you can always reach the destination in two moves by first moving to a cell whose row differs from both sx and ex and whose column differs from both sy and ey (the 10×10 grid guarantees such a cell exists). Hence the answer is 2.
The algorithm is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.
